post.title: Rice Surplus: A Balance-Sheet Risk, not a Food-Security Trophy!!
post.excerpt: India’s rice story has been framed as proof of strength: record procurement, bulging stocks, and “we can feed the world.” But in 2026, we need to say the quiet part out loud: When rice stocks spill beyond buffer norms, surplus stops being security—and starts...
post.body:
India’s rice story has been framed as proof of strength: record procurement, bulging stocks, and “we can feed the world.” But in 2026, we need to say the quiet part out loud: When rice stocks spill beyond buffer norms, surplus stops being security—and starts becoming a balance-sheet liability. Because a “surplus” is not free. It sits on the public balance sheet as: Working capital locked for months (and sometimes years) Interest + storage + handling costs that quietly balloon into thousands of crores Quality deterioration risk (time is not kind to grain) Logistics choke points that paralyse the next crop cycle Miller distress, when the system’s cash cycle breaks and by-products don’t clear This is the paradox: we celebrate procurement like a trophy, then wonder why godowns are choking, mills look unviable, and export offers soften when global buyers hesitate. Food security is not measured by “how much we can store.” It must be measured by how efficiently we can rotate—from farmgate to consumer, from inventory to nutrition, from stock to stability. The new operating principle: Rotate fast. Don’t hoard. If India wants surplus without stress, policy must shift from accumulation to asset management. What immediate intervention should look like (not slogans): 1) Create space—fast Use OMSS aggressively with realistic clearing prices. Old stock must move before wheat pressure and before quality cost escalates. 2) Convert inventory to cash or kind. Surplus grain is a financial asset only when it can be monetised. Scale structured channels: institutional offtake, welfare distribution efficiency, and credible G2G/humanitarian pipelines.

3) Protect the milling ecosystem. Mills are not “middlemen”; they are processing infrastructure. Fix payment cycles, reduce procedural shocks, and make CMR/movement timelines predictable. A distressed milling system is how surplus becomes waste. 4) Stop re-forming the surplus. Procurement must be smarter, not bigger—especially in chronic- surplus geographies. Back diversification with hard incentives (pulses, oilseeds, maize), not just advisories. The hard truth A large buffer is strategic. A permanent mountain is expensive. If we treat surplus as a trophy, we will keep paying for storage, interest, and inefficiency. If we treat it as a balance-sheet risk, we will design policies that optimises rotation, liquidity, and resilience. In 2026, the goal is minimum carrying cost per unit of food security. That’s how a surplus becomes strength—without becoming a burden.
post.author: Know Your Commodity
